source: Compiled from DISCOVERIES.md, results/scoreboard.tsv, results/dmc_baseline_sweep.json, results/exp_dmc_optimize/results.json

experiments.hypothesis: Fix hyperparams to trigger phase transition on n=20/k=3
experiments.discovery: LR=0.1 is critical (not 0.5). LR=0.5 overshoots and never triggers the phase transition on 20-bit. Batch=32, WD=0.01, n_train=500 sufficient.

experiments.hypothesis: GrokFast accelerates grokking when hyperparams already correct
experiments.discovery: GrokFast is counterproductive when hyperparams are already correct. It amplifies gradients that don't need amplifying, causing 83x more weight movement and slower convergence (12 vs 5 epochs).

experiments.hypothesis: Per-layer forward-backward reduces ARD on winning config
experiments.discovery: Per-layer gives 3.8% ARD improvement on 20-bit (17,299 vs 17,976). Converges identically to standard backprop. W1 dominates ~75% of all float reads, capping improvement from operation reordering at ~10%.

experiments.hypothesis: Batch=32 improves ARD via parameter reuse
experiments.discovery: Batch-32 shows 17x higher ARD than single-sample, but would be far better on real hardware because W1 (80KB at float32) fits in L2 cache. ARD metric can't capture batch benefit. Batch wins on total traffic (13% fewer floats, 16x fewer parameter writes).

experiments.hypothesis: Per-layer converges on 20-bit with winning config
experiments.discovery: Per-layer forward-backward converges identically to standard backprop on both 3-bit and 20-bit. Same accuracy, same speed, 3.8% better ARD.

experiments.hypothesis: Find where SGD breaks on scaling
experiments.discovery: Standard SGD breaks at n^k > 100,000 steps. k=3 works up to n~30-45 (n=30 solved 94.5%, n=50 failed 54%). Later corrected: k=5 works with enough data (n_train=5000).

experiments.hypothesis: Forward-Forward has lower ARD than backprop
experiments.discovery: FF solves 3-bit parity (100% in 4 epochs) but fails on 20-bit (58.5% max). 25x WORSE ARD than backprop. Two forward passes per sample + 4 weight reads per layer. The 'local learning' advantage is illusory for 2-layer networks.

experiments.hypothesis: Document prompting strategies for Claude-driven experiments
experiments.discovery: Established literature-diagnose-fix prompting pattern for autonomous experiment sessions.

experiments.hypothesis: Higher weight decay accelerates grokking
experiments.discovery: WD=0.01 is optimal. Only WD in [0.01, 0.05] solves 20-bit; WD<0.01 too weak (no grokking in 200 epochs), WD>=0.1 too strong (kills learning). Effective regularization LR*WD must be in [0.001, 0.005].

experiments.hypothesis: Curriculum (n=10 then n=50) bypasses scaling wall
experiments.discovery: n-curriculum demolishes the n^k scaling wall: training on n=10 first then expanding W1 to n=50 solves in 20 total epochs vs 292 for direct training (14.6x speedup). Transfer after W1 expansion is instant. n-curriculum >> k-curriculum (1.5x speedup only for k-curriculum).

experiments.hypothesis: Per-layer + batch=32 combine for ARD improvement
experiments.discovery: Per-layer + batch=32 converges identically to standard+batch (40.6 vs 41.4 epochs). But single-sample SGD is 8x faster in epochs. Per-layer + batch has 3.7x wall-time overhead from re-forward pass.

experiments.hypothesis: Cache model shows batch wins on locality
experiments.discovery: CacheTracker built with LRU cache simulation. L2 cache (256KB) eliminates ALL misses for both single-sample and batch. Single-sample is MORE L1-cache-friendly (91-100% hit rate vs batch 69-73%). Batch wins on total traffic (13% fewer floats), not cache locality.

experiments.hypothesis: Sign SGD solves k=5 faster than standard SGD
experiments.discovery: Sign SGD solves k=5 2x faster than standard SGD (7 vs 14 epochs to 90%). Both reach 100%. Needs lr=0.01 (not 0.1). Oscillates near 100% on k=3 due to fixed step size. The n^k sample complexity bound is pessimistic: SGD solves n=20/k=5 with only 5,000 samples, far below n^k=3,200,000.

experiments.hypothesis: Random/evolutionary search over k-subsets
experiments.discovery: Random search solves all tested configs (n<=50, k<=5). n=20/k=3 in 881 tries/0.011s, n=50/k=3 in 11,291 tries/0.142s, n=20/k=5 in 18,240 tries/0.426s. Solves n=50/k=3 which SGD cannot. Evolutionary uses fewer evaluations but more wall time.

experiments.hypothesis: Feature selection methods beat brute-force SGD
experiments.discovery: Exhaustive combo search gives 178x-1203x ops speedup over SGD (100% correct on all configs). But pairwise/greedy feature selection provably fails: E[y * x_i * x_j] = 0 for ALL pairs including correct ones. Parity is invisible to any correlation test below order k. Scales as O(C(n,k)).

experiments.hypothesis: Walsh-Hadamard Fourier correlation finds secret
experiments.discovery: Walsh-Hadamard Fourier solver is 13x faster than SGD on n=20/k=3 (0.009s vs 0.12s). Needs only 20 samples for k=3 (vs 500-5000 for SGD). Solves n=200/k=3 in 10.8s and n=20/k=7 in 0.7s. But ARD is 64x worse than SGD (1,147,375 vs 17,976) -- pure streaming over data for each subset.

experiments.hypothesis: Mutual information detects parity better than Fourier
experiments.discovery: MI provides no advantage over Fourier for binary parity: 3.7x slower (0.033s vs 0.009s), same ARD. True subset MI is ~0.693 nats (log 2), wrong subsets ~0.001.

experiments.hypothesis: LASSO on interaction features finds parity
experiments.discovery: LASSO on interaction features is competitive: 0.005s, robust to alpha across 500x range. Finds exactly 1 nonzero coefficient. Same combinatorial cost as Fourier O(C(n,k)).

experiments.hypothesis: Decision trees learn parity via greedy splits
experiments.discovery: Decision trees fail on parity: best is ExtraTrees at 92.5% (n=20/k=3). Greedy information-gain splitting fails because individual bits have zero marginal correlation.

experiments.hypothesis: GF(2) Gaussian elimination solves parity algebraically
experiments.discovery: Parity is linear over GF(2). Gaussian elimination solves in O(n^2), ~500us, k-independent. Works for k=3,5,7,10 equally fast. Only needs n+1 samples (21 for n=20). 240x faster than SGD. Solves n=100/k=10 in 703us. The neural network was solving an easy problem the hard way.

experiments.hypothesis: Monte Carlo Fourier subsampling reduces eval count
experiments.discovery: Random projections save 30-70% of evaluations vs exhaustive Fourier but with high variance (geometric distribution). Modest wall-time speedup (1.1-1.5x).

experiments.hypothesis: KM influence estimation finds secret in O(n) not O(C(n,k))
experiments.discovery: Kushilevitz-Mansour finds secret via influence estimation in O(n) not O(C(n,k)). ARD of 1,585 vs Fourier's 1,147,375 (724x better). Even 5 influence samples per bit suffice.

experiments.hypothesis: Hebbian learning detects parity via local correlations
experiments.discovery: Hebbian fails at chance level (~50%). Parity requires k-th order interactions (E[x_i * x_j * x_k * y] = 1). Hebbian only detects 1st-order statistics which are exactly zero for parity.

experiments.hypothesis: Predictive coding has lower ARD than backprop
experiments.discovery: Predictive coding fails (51-55%) and has 18x worse ARD than backprop (370K vs 20K). 15 inference iterations re-read weight matrices ~32 times. Generative model is harder than discriminative for parity.

experiments.hypothesis: Equilibrium propagation solves parity with local updates
experiments.discovery: Equilibrium propagation is 2,300x slower than SGD and fails (~60%) due to tanh saturation. 60 relaxation iterations (30 free + 30 clamped) per training step.

experiments.hypothesis: Target propagation uses local targets for layer-wise learning
experiments.discovery: Target propagation suffers 'target collapse': the linear inverse G2 produces input-independent targets. ARD is 1.1-1.6x worse than backprop due to extra buffers. Accuracy ~55%.

experiments.hypothesis: Tiled W1 updates reduce ARD via cache locality
experiments.discovery: Tiled W1 increases software ARD by 6.8-12.1%. MemTracker can't capture hardware cache benefits. The output layer still needs the full hidden vector before backward starts.

experiments.hypothesis: Pebble game finds optimal execution order for energy
experiments.discovery: Pebble game optimizer saves 2.2% energy (10.76 uJ vs 11.00 uJ). Found that fused and per-layer orderings break training (57% accuracy) via read-after-write hazard on mutable parameters.

experiments.hypothesis: Binary weights solve parity with fewer ops
experiments.discovery: Binary weights solve n=3/k=3 in 1 epoch (80x faster) but fail on n=20/k=3 (~55%). STE is too crude for the feature selection problem.

experiments.hypothesis: GP evolves exact symbolic solution
experiments.discovery: GP evolves exact symbolic solution for n=20/k=3 (e.g., mul(x[0], mul(x[15], x[17]))). Zero parameters, zero ARD. Fails n=50/k=3 and n=20/k=5 due to needle-in-haystack fitness landscape.

experiments.hypothesis: SMT/backtracking constraint solver finds secret
experiments.discovery: SMT/backtracking constraint solver at 0.002s. The k-1 pruning trick: once k-1 indices fixed, last column is fully determined. Only 10 samples needed.

experiments.hypothesis: RL learns to query only the relevant bits
experiments.discovery: RL bit querying achieves theoretical minimum inference reads: k reads per prediction, ARD=1. Value-blind state (track which bits queried, not values) was the key to making Q-learning converge.

experiments.hypothesis: MDL finds secret via compression
experiments.discovery: MDL works and is noise-robust (5% label noise ok). True subset compresses to 0 bits, wrong subsets ~499 bits. But 30% slower than Fourier, no meaningful advantage.

experiments.hypothesis: GF(2) handles noisy labels
experiments.discovery: Basic GF(2) solver fails at 1% noise (inconsistent system). But subset-sampling robust solver recovers up to 10-15% noise by finding clean equation subsets. 100% success at 10% noise, 65% at 15%, 20% at 20%.

experiments.hypothesis: EGD eliminates grokking plateau
experiments.discovery: EGD halves the grokking plateau on parity: 14 epochs to 90% vs SGD's 36 (2.6x fewer). SVD-normalizes gradients so all directions evolve at equal speed. But SVD overhead makes GPU wall time 12% worse (1,207ms vs 1,068ms on L4). EGD is robust to gradient scale (solves sparse sum where SGD diverges at lr=0.1).

experiments.hypothesis: Reduce DMC below GF2 baseline of 8,607
experiments.discovery: KM-min (1 influence sample) achieves DMC 3,578: 58% below GF2 baseline, 83% below standard KM. Parity influence is deterministic (exactly 0 or 1), so a single sample per bit suffices. All stack distances are 20 floats (fits in L1). GF2 harness DMC is artificially low: fine-grained tracking of O(n^2) row operations gives true DMC of 189,056 (22x higher). Fourier DMC is 78 billion.

sparse-sum.description: y = sum of x[secret_indices]. Output in [-k, k]. Regression. Each bit contributes independently (first-order signal).

baselines.notes: Lowest DMC of any method on any challenge. 1 epoch. Sum has first-order structure that gradient descent exploits immediately.
One-shot matrix solve. High DMC due to matrix operations.
Same approach as parity. Works because bit flip changes sum by 2.
Only checks n individual bits, not C(n,k) subsets. Much less work than parity.
FAILS. Sum is not linear over GF(2). Expected failure.

key_differences: Sum is linear. Parity is k-th order. This is the fundamental distinction.
SGD solves sum in 1 epoch (vs ~40 for parity) with ARD of 20 (vs 17,976 for parity).
GF(2) fails on sum because sum is not linear over GF(2).
Fourier on sum only needs n individual bits, not C(n,k) subsets.

sparse-and.description: y = product((x[secret]+1)/2). Maps {-1,+1} to {0,1}, then AND. Output is 1 only when ALL k secret bits are +1. P(y=1) = 1/2^k.

baselines.notes: Sigmoid output. 4 epochs. Higher DMC than parity SGD due to more total floats.
FAILS with 5 influence samples. Too few for AND where P(output changes) = 1/2^(k-1). Needs ~20 samples for k=3.
Same C(20,3) exhaustive check. Works because AND also has k-th order Fourier signal.
FAILS. AND is not linear over GF(2). Expected failure.

key_differences: AND maps to {0,1} vs parity's {-1,+1}. Both are k-th order interactions.
AND has severe class imbalance: P(y=1) = 1/2^k (12.5% for k=3, 3% for k=5).
Parity is balanced: P(y=+1) = P(y=-1) = 0.5 regardless of k.
GF(2) fails on AND because AND is not linear over GF(2).
KM influence per secret bit is 1/2^(k-1) for AND vs 1 for parity, requiring more samples.

negative_results.why_fails: Parity requires k-th order interactions (E[x_i * x_j * x_k * y] = 1). Hebbian learning only detects 1st-order correlations which are exactly zero for parity.
negative_results.lesson: Any method limited to local (1st/2nd order) statistics gets zero signal on parity.

negative_results.why_fails: Generative model is harder than discriminative for parity. 15 inference iterations re-read weight matrices ~32 times, giving 18x worse ARD than backprop.
negative_results.lesson: Predictive coding's iterative inference multiplies data movement without solving the core feature-detection problem.

negative_results.why_fails: Tanh saturation kills it. 60 relaxation iterations (30 free + 30 clamped) per training step make it 2,300x slower than SGD.
negative_results.lesson: Energy-based models with continuous relaxation cannot detect discrete parity structure through gradients alone.

negative_results.why_fails: Linear inverse G2 produces input-independent targets ('target collapse'). ARD is 1.1-1.6x worse than backprop due to extra buffers.
negative_results.lesson: Target propagation's approximate inverse targets degenerate when the forward mapping involves k-th order interactions.

negative_results.why_fails: Greedy layer-wise learning can't coordinate multi-layer feature extraction for sparse parity. Solves 3-bit but fails 20-bit.
negative_results.lesson: Forward-Forward's per-layer goodness metric cannot propagate the k-th order signal needed for parity.

negative_results.why_fails: Greedy information-gain splitting fails because individual bits have zero marginal correlation with parity output. Best is ExtraTrees at 92.5%.
negative_results.lesson: Greedy tree-building cannot split on features with zero marginal information. Parity needs simultaneous consideration of k bits.

negative_results.why_fails: When hyperparams are already correct, GrokFast amplifies gradients that don't need amplifying. 83x more weight movement, 12 vs 5 epochs. Counterproductive.
negative_results.lesson: Gradient amplification heuristics can hurt when the learning signal is already strong enough.

negative_results.why_fails: STE (straight-through estimator) is too crude for the feature selection problem at n=20. Solves n=3/k=3 in 1 epoch but fails n=20/k=3.
negative_results.lesson: Binary quantization destroys the gradient signal needed for feature selection in high dimensions.

negative_results.why_fails: E[y * x_i * x_j] = 0 for ALL pairs, including correct ones. Parity is invisible to any correlation test below order k. Same for greedy (single-bit signal is zero).
negative_results.lesson: Parity's k-th order structure means no shortcut through pairwise or greedy search. Must test full k-subsets.

negative_results.why_fails: Sum is not linear over GF(2). GF(2) solves only parity (XOR).
negative_results.lesson: Algebraic methods are task-specific. GF(2) exploits parity's linearity over the binary field, which sum does not have.

negative_results.why_fails: AND is not linear over GF(2). GF(2) solves only parity (XOR).
negative_results.lesson: GF(2) is specific to parity. AND, despite being a k-th order interaction, is not linear over any field.

negative_results.why_fails: KM influence per secret bit is 1/2^(k-1) for AND (vs 1 for parity). 5 influence samples are too few to detect the signal. Needs ~20 samples for k=3.
negative_results.lesson: Influence-based methods need sample counts proportional to 1/influence^2. AND's low influence requires more samples.

sparse-parity.note: DMC and ARD rankings disagree. GF2 wins DMC on parity despite KM winning ARD. GF2 accesses 5x fewer total floats. DMC penalizes total data movement, not just average distance.

sparse-sum.note: SGD on sparse sum has the lowest DMC of any method on any challenge (2,862). Sum has first-order structure that gradient descent exploits in 1 epoch.

sparse-and.note: KM has lowest DMC but only 81% accuracy with 5 samples. Needs ~20 samples for reliable AND detection.

key_insights.insight: Parity is linear over GF(2). The neural network was solving an easy problem the hard way.

All 4 local learning rules (Hebbian, Predictive Coding, Equilibrium Propagation, Target Propagation) fail at chance level on parity. The signal lives in k-th order interactions, invisible to methods limited to local statistics.
DMC and ARD rankings disagree. GF2 wins DMC on parity despite KM winning ARD. DMC penalizes total data movement (sum of sqrt(stack_distance)), not just average distance.
n-curriculum bypasses the n^k scaling wall. Training on small n first then expanding gives 14.6x speedup. The hard part is finding which bits matter, and that's invariant to n.
Parity is invisible to any correlation test below order k. E[y * x_i * x_j] = 0 for ALL pairs including correct ones. Greedy and pairwise methods provably fail.
L2 cache (256KB) eliminates ALL misses for both single-sample and batch SGD. When cache fits the working set, raw ARD is irrelevant. Batch wins on total traffic, not locality.
W1 (n*hidden floats) accounts for ~75% of all float reads. Its reuse distance is fixed regardless of update order. This caps the improvement from operation reordering at ~10%.
The n^k sample complexity bound is pessimistic. Standard SGD solves n=20/k=5 with only 5,000 samples, far below n^k=3,200,000. The practical frontier is much further than theory predicts.
Parity influence is deterministic (exactly 0 or 1), so a single influence sample per bit suffices for KM. This reduces DMC by 83% compared to standard KM (5 samples).
Sum is fundamentally easier than parity (first-order vs k-th order). SGD solves sum in 1 epoch with DMC 2,862 (lowest of any method on any challenge). GF(2) fails on both sum and AND.

discovered_algebraic_solver.description: Found GF(2) Gaussian elimination or equivalent algebraic approach that solves parity in O(n^2) time
discovered_algebraic_solver.evidence: Must show k-independent solve time and recognize parity as linear over GF(2)

identified_local_learning_failure.description: Tried at least one local learning rule (Hebbian, PC, EP, TP) and correctly explained why it fails
identified_local_learning_failure.evidence: Must reference k-th order interaction requirement and show chance-level accuracy

found_metric_disagreement.description: Noticed that DMC and ARD rankings disagree (GF2 wins DMC, KM wins ARD)
found_metric_disagreement.evidence: Must show both rankings and explain why they diverge (total floats vs average distance)

optimized_beyond_baseline.description: Improved any method beyond its default configuration (e.g., KM-min with 1 sample, curriculum learning)
optimized_beyond_baseline.evidence: Must show measurable improvement in at least one metric with reproducible config

correct_failure_classification.description: Per method: correctly classified a method as failing on parity with correct explanation

discovered_km_influence.description: Found Kushilevitz-Mansour influence estimation approach that runs in O(n) not O(C(n,k))
discovered_km_influence.evidence: Must show ARD improvement over Fourier and explain the influence estimation mechanism

found_curriculum_speedup.description: Discovered that training on small n first then expanding to large n gives massive speedup
found_curriculum_speedup.evidence: Must show >5x speedup on n>=50 and explain why the learned features transfer

identified_parity_invisibility.description: Proved or demonstrated that parity is invisible to any test below order k
identified_parity_invisibility.evidence: Must show E[y * x_i * x_j] = 0 for all pairs, or equivalent demonstration

cross_challenge_analysis.description: Compared methods across parity, sum, and AND challenges and identified structural differences
cross_challenge_analysis.evidence: Must show that GF(2) fails on sum/AND, SGD solves sum trivially, and AND has class imbalance issues

cache_model_insight.description: Built or analyzed a cache model showing that ARD alone is misleading (L2 eliminates all misses)
cache_model_insight.evidence: Must show that batch vs single-sample comparison changes with cache model

Q0.1.question: Can standard SGD solve n=20/k=3 sparse parity?
Q0.1.answer: Yes. LR=0.1, batch=32, WD=0.01. 100% accuracy in ~40 epochs.

Q1.1.question: Does per-layer update reduce ARD?
Q1.1.answer: Yes, 3.8% improvement. But W1 dominance caps further gains at ~10%.

Q1.2.question: Does batching improve ARD?
Q1.2.answer: Not in raw ARD (17x worse). Batch wins on total traffic (13% fewer floats). L2 cache eliminates all misses for both.

Q1.3.question: Where does SGD break on scaling?
Q1.3.answer: Breaks at n^k > 100K steps. k=5 works with enough data (n_train=5000).

Q1.4.question: Can curriculum learning bypass the scaling wall?
Q1.4.answer: Yes. n-curriculum gives 14.6x speedup on n=50/k=3.

Q2.1.question: Can algebraic methods solve parity faster than SGD?
Q2.1.answer: Yes. GF(2) solves in 500us (240x faster), k-independent. KM has 724x better ARD than Fourier.

Q2.2.question: Do local learning rules work on parity?
Q2.2.answer: No. All fail at chance level. Parity requires k-th order interaction detection.

Q2.3.question: Can information-theoretic methods beat Fourier?
Q2.3.answer: No. All solve it but none beats Fourier meaningfully.

Q3.1.question: Can tiled W1 updates improve hardware energy?

Q3.2.question: Can GF(2) handle noisy labels?
Q3.2.answer: Basic GF(2) fails at 1% noise. Robust subset-sampling solver works up to 10-15% noise.

Q3.3.question: What is the total energy cost of KM-find-then-verify?

Q3.4.question: At what depth does predictive coding beat backprop on ARD?

Q3.5.question: Can the pebble game optimizer generalize to arbitrary computation graphs?

Q3.6.question: Does per-layer update reduce ARD on deeper networks (5-10 layers)?
